messages.content: Write a forum convo where the radio station Great Radio has left 231 AM at midday, First there was a goodbye to listeners and then a final ad break, which segued into a fixed loop of songs (Bad to the Bone, Sweet Home Alabama, Paint it Black, Seven Nation Army, Rasputin and Gangstas/Amish Paradise) This is interspersed with a message advising people that "Normal transmissions on this frequency have now drawn to a close, and we urge you to retune to 23.4 FM to continue listening, Or go to greatradio.co.uk, Goodbye to this frequency"

Title: Great Radio's Move from 231 AM to 23.4 FM: Share Your Thoughts!

User1: DJMike
Posted: Today at 12:05 PM
Hey fellow Great Radio listeners! Just tuned in to catch the last moments on 231 AM, and I gotta say, it was pretty emotional. That goodbye message hit right in the feels. Anyone else catch it?

User2: ClassicTunesLover
Posted: Today at 12:10 PM
@DJMike Yeah, it was surreal. I've been listening on this frequency since I was in high school. Can't believe it's the end of an era. But hey, change is inevitable, right? That last ad break was a classic Great Radio move, though.

User3: VinylFanatic
Posted: Today at 12:12 PM
Definitely the end of an era! Remember the days when FM was the new kid on the block and AM ruled the airwaves? Technical progress, my friends. Anyway, that song loop they left us with is kinda iconic. A fitting tribute if you ask me!

User1: DJMike
Posted: Today at 12:14 PM
@ClassicTunesLover, @VinylFanatic Couldn't agree more! "Bad to the Bone" going into "Sweet Home Alabama" gave me chills. I think they meant "Stairway to Heaven" when they planned that loop, haha! 🤘

User4: NewWaveNancy
Posted: Today at 12:17 PM
I tuned in just as "Paint it Black" was playing. Missed the goodbye announcement but caught the looping message about moving to 23.4 FM. I've already retuned all my receivers at home and in the car. Ready for the switch!

User2: ClassicTunesLover
Posted: Today at 12:20 PM
@NewWaveNancy Smart move! Also, guys, don't forget about their online streaming at greatradio.co.uk. I bet they've been prepping that service up for this move. It's all about the digital age!

User5: ThePrankster
Posted: Today at 12:22 PM
Am I the only one who chuckled when they went from "Gangsta's Paradise" into "Amish Paradise"? 😂 Classic Great Radio humor in those final moments. But dang, definitely gonna miss this station on the AM dial.

User3: VinylFanatic
Posted: Today at 12:25 PM
@ThePrankster No, you're not alone! Thought the mix was spot on—paying homage to the classics and having a laugh too. It's sad to see them go from AM, but I'm excited to see what they'll do on FM.

User6: RadioRenegade
Posted: Today at 12:30 PM
Caught that farewell on my lunch break. Solid choice of tunes to ride out the last wave on 231 AM. This isn't a goodbye, just a retune, right? I'm looking forward to crisper sound on 23.4 FM. Time marches on, people!

User1: DJMike
Posted: Today at 12:35 PM
So, anyone planning a listening party for the first broadcast on 23.4 FM? I think it deserves some celebration!

User4: NewWaveNancy
Posted: Today at 12:40 PM
@DJMike Count me in for that listening party! Let's kick off this new beginning with some good vibes. Can't wait to hear how Great Radio sounds on FM!

User7: AMforLife
Posted: Today at 12:45 PM
Just checked back after a break from the forum to find this news. I'm all for FM, but there was something special about the AM static and knowing it was reaching across the miles. Will definitely miss it but looking forward to the new chapter with Great Radio._SIGNAL FOREVER!_

User1: DJMike
Posted: Today at 12:50 PM
@AMforLife Indeed, there's a unique charm to AM signals, but I think we've got great things ahead on FM. Plus, with their online streaming, Great Radio is never really out of reach. Here's to tuning in and turning up, folks! 📻🎶

(Following replies continue discussing memories of 231 AM, expectations for 23.4 FM, and planning a virtual listening party for the community to celebrate the transition.)

Write a follow up a few weeks later when the loop ends, but the TX is still on with engineering announcements from Ofcom, saying "Ofcom regulates TV and mobile, landlines telephone and mobile, plus the airwaves used by wireless devices" and it talks about what Ofcom do, and says the frequency was recently vacated, and it ends by advising us to go to ofcom.co.uk and then also directing us to careers.ofcom.co.uk, After this there was a similar announcement from Arqiva, The goal of these announcements are quite obvious, Also they are ceasing from midnight

Title: The End of An Era: 231 AM Officially Ceases Broadcasting

User8: FrequencyFinder
Posted: 3 weeks later at 11:00 AM
Hey everyone, I've been tuned in to the old 231 AM frequency out of habit, and it looks like the loop of classics we had for the past few weeks finally came to an end this morning. Now there's just a continuous loop of Ofcom and Arqiva engineering announcements talking about their roles. Looks like they want to make sure everyone knows the frequency's been vacated.

User4: NewWaveNancy
Posted: 3 weeks later at 11:05 AM
@FrequencyFinder I heard that too! The Ofcom announcement is quite clear about their regulatory role for TV, phones, and airwaves. It's kind of sad knowing that there won't be any music or shows on that frequency anymore.

User9: CallSignEnthusiast
Posted: 3 weeks later at 11:10 AM
Yeah, it's pretty much official now. The announcements from Ofcom and Arqiva sound like they're wrapping things up and freeing up the space for potential new uses. They mentioned careers too, seems like a bit of a recruitment push? Either way, I guess it's smart of them to use the remaining air time for something informational.

User10: DigitalShiftPro
Posted: 3 weeks later at 11:15 AM
Just tuned in to catch these announcements. It’s a practical move—Ofcom providing awareness about who they are and what they do, plus directing folks to more information and job opportunities. Clear sign they want to repurpose the frequency. Arqiva's message seems in line with their typical infrastructure and broadcast support role.

User1: DJMike
Posted: 3 weeks later at 11:20 AM
Caught those closing announcements too. Sounds like last-minute housekeeping to me. But they said all transmissions would cease by midnight, right? Guess we’ll have dead air by tomorrow.

User5: ThePrankster
Posted: 3 weeks later at 11:25 AM
@DJMike, yeah, that's the word. Full stop on 231 AM as we know it. Feels a little like part of my daily routine is being taken away. Still, you can't stop progress, can you?

User11: AirwaveAdmirer
Posted: 3 weeks later at 11:30 AM
It's smart of organizations like Ofcom and Arqiva to use the last breath of the frequency for some kind of public service and outreach. Maybe some radio enthusiasts here might discover a new career path thanks to these announcements?

User7: AMforLife
Posted: 3 weeks later at 11:35 AM
I've been following Ofcom’s work for a while, so hearing them on the old Great Radio frequency was kind of bittersweet. But yeah, the writing's been on the wall, and it’s probably time to fully let go and embrace the new ways we consume media.

User12: TechWizard
Posted: 3 weeks later at 11:40 AM
It’s a clear sign the frequency is being decommissioned. As for the announcements, I bet Ofcom and Arqiva are advertising the vacated space and their services to potential buyers or leasers. Clever marketing ploy under the guise of informational messages.

User4: NewWaveNancy
Posted: 3 weeks later at 11:45 AM
Well, I'll be following Ofcom's advice and shutting down my trusty AM receiver after tonight. I guess it's finally time to bid farewell to 231 AM. 📡💔

(Following replies continue discussing the impact of the frequency shutdown, reminiscing on the shared memories of the Great Radio era on 231 AM, and speculating on the future use of the frequency.)

Write a follow up after midnight where an echoed Goodbye plays, then dead air for a few seconds before a Hello, plays coming from Krash the Rabbit from Kikoriki, who then says he will be doing funny stuff on this frequency for the "foreseeable" The Krash comedy is a 30 minute loop of jokes, a fake conversation, and playing

Title: Surprise Twist on 231 AM: Krash the Rabbit Hijacks the Airwaves!

User13: NightOwl
Posted: After Midnight at 12:10 AM
Whoa, did anyone else hear that just now? Right after midnight, there was this echoey "Goodbye" on 231 AM, then dead air that made me think it was all over. But suddenly, Krash the Rabbit from Kikoriki popped up with a "Hello!" This is insane. He said he'll be doing comedy stuff on this frequency for the "foreseeable future." I'm not even mad; this is hilarious!

User14: LateNightComedyFan
Posted: After Midnight at 12:15 AM
@NightOwl, Yes! This is so random! I was about to switch off my radio for good when "Hello!" nearly gave me a heart attack. Krash's comedy loop is actually pretty funny. A 30-minute run of jokes, fake convos, and playacting. Wonder how long this is going to last?

User1: DJMike
Posted: After Midnight at 12:20 AM
Haha, glad I wasn't dreaming! That transition from dead air to Krash the Rabbit was too good. I can't believe the 231 AM frequency is now home to a comedic cartoon character. Maybe it's some sort of pirate radio stunt, or is Great Radio behind this?

User15: KikoLover
Posted: After Midnight at 12:25 AM
OMG, I LOVE Kikoriki! Never thought I'd hear Krash's voice on the radio waves like this. Whoever set this up is a genius. When he said "foreseeable," I bet it's a temporary thing. But I’m gonna enjoy it while it lasts.

User8: FrequencyFinder
Posted: After Midnight at 12:30 AM
This is either a really clever guerrilla marketing campaign, or maybe someone at the station is having one last hurrah. 231 AM was going out with a whisper, but now it's a bang!

User16: PrankOrTruth
Posted: After Midnight at 12:40 AM
What’s going on?! This has to be some kind of prank. Right when we all thought it was the end for 231 AM, out comes a cartoon rabbit! This "Krash comedy" thing is just bizarre enough to make you wonder who's really behind it.

User17: RadioSleuth
Posted: After Midnight at 12:50 AM
Could this be the new lease of life for the 231 AM slot? Krash the Rabbit doing a comedy shtick on a supposedly dead frequency is definitely worth tuning in for. However, I’m really curious to see how this plays out with Ofcom, given the previously announced shutdown.

User18: TheConspiracyKid
Posted: After Midnight at 1:00 AM
Hold up, everyone... What if this isn't just a random event? Could it be an easter egg for something bigger coming to the frequency? Maybe they're testing the waters to see the audience's reaction. Krash could just be the beginning!

User5: ThePrankster
Posted: After Midnight at 1:10 AM
Caught the tail end of the Krash loop. Hilarious stuff! No better way to go out (or come back in?) than with a good laugh. 231 AM still has some tricks up its sleeve, it seems!

(Following replies continue discussing the unexpected arrival of Krash the Rabbit on the 231 AM frequency, theorizing about the reasons behind this surprise broadcast.)
Write a short tweet from about the phases of the closedown of Great, and the bridge between the "Song Filled Goodbye" and "Fun Filled Krash" loops

Tweet from @RadioChatter:

📻 As the curtain falls on #GreatRadio 231 AM, we reminisce the journey from the heartfelt #SongFilledGoodbye 🎶 to the unexpected arrival of a #FunFilledKrash 🐰. In the static between, memories echo, frequencies shift, and laughter emerges. What a wild ride for the airwaves! #RadioDays

Write a forum convo a few months later where he is still keeping the seat warm, but his messages are replaced every 3 days with a new set of fun, Also, rumors are that it might begin relaying BBC One (but audio)

Title: Krash the Rabbit Still Rocking 231 AM – Plus, BBC One Rumors?

User19: KrashFan101
Posted: A few months later at 10:00 AM
Can you believe it’s been months and Krash the Rabbit is still "keeping the seat warm" on 231 AM? His hilarious skits are the highlight of my drive. And every 3 days there's something new. How long do you think he’ll keep this up?

User4: NewWaveNancy
Posted: A few months later at 10:05 AM
@KrashFan101 I know, right? I'm impressed with the consistency and fresh content. I thought it'd be a one-time thing, but here we are. I secretly hope Krash stays around – his antics are a real treat!

User20: BroadcastBuzz
Posted: A few months later at 10:10 AM
Whispers are going around that 231 AM might start relaying BBC One audio. Ever since that "Goodbye" and the Krash takeover, I’ve been waiting for the next big surprise. Anyone else heard these rumors?

User21: AirwavesAnalyst
Posted: A few months later at 10:15 AM
@BroadcastBuzz That would be interesting if true. Relaying BBC One audio could be a smart way to repurpose the frequency. I wonder how Krash fits into the long-term picture though. Interim entertainment before the big change?

User13: NightOwl
Posted: A few months later at 10:20 AM
That rumor has some weight to it. BBC One audio on AM could pull in a different listening crowd. Krash has been great, but I’m not sure if it was ever meant to be permanent. Still, the rabbit's got staying power!

User22: TheRadioHistorian
Posted: A few months later at 10:30 AM
I'm intrigued by the BBC One idea – it could serve as a nostalgia throwback when TV audio on the radio was a thing. Krash's tenure has been unprecedented and delightful, but the BBC move? That could bring 231 AM back into the mainstream in a new way.

User5: ThePrankster
Posted: A few months later at 10:35 AM
If Krash is just a placeholder until the BBC One switchover, I’ll miss our furry comedian. The guy’s a hoot! But hey, I’d tune in for some BBC content too. Either way, 231 AM is far from dead and buried.

User23: SitcomSally
Posted: A few months later at 10:40 AM
Imagine catching up with "EastEnders" or "The Great British Bake Off" on the radio! Could be cozy in its own vintage radio drama way. Krash brought the laughs, but BBC content has its own charm.

User24: FrequencyNostalgic
Posted: A few months later at 10:45 AM
It's wild how 231 AM has transformed. From classic tunes to cartoon comedy, and now possibly BBC audio? What a trip! Krash will always be a part of that quirky history now. But moving forward with BBC? Count me in!

User25: ComedyCraze
Posted: A few months later at 10:50 AM
Don’t get me wrong, I’d listen to BBC One. But Krash has become like that oddball friend you never knew you needed. His ever-changing jokes and skits? Radio gold! Hoping he isn’t going anywhere soon!

(Following replies continue discussing the surprise longevity of Krash the Rabbit's presence on 231 AM, the possibility of BBC One audio taking over the frequency, and whether the change is temporary or a sign of permanent programming shift.)

Write a follow up where Nart Media submit a notice to Ofcom about "changing the format of the station, which up untill May was a predominantly music based service, The license was changed to a placeholder format, a Ofcom recruitment drive message filled the gap between formats", and also that the format would transmit S4C audio between 6am and 12pm, BBC One between 12pm and 9pm and the Krash audio between 9pm and 6am

Title: Big Changes Ahead for 231 AM: Nart Media Submits New Format Notice to Ofcom

User26: AMWaveWatcher
Posted: Date at 09:00 AM
Heads up, radio lovers! Nart Media has just sent a notice to Ofcom proposing a complete format change for our beloved 231 AM. Since May, when it was mostly tunes we loved, they've held a placeholder format. Remember the Ofcom recruitment loops? Well, it seems like that was just the bridge to something bigger.

User27: MorningNewsHound
Posted: Date at 09:05 AM
Just read about the Nart Media notice. They’re planning a diverse lineup! We're looking at S4C audio broadcasts from 6am to 12pm – a great slot for Welsh language content. Interesting decision to change from the predominantly music-based service. 

User28: PrimeTimeListener
Posted: Date at 09:10 AM
Wow, S4C in the morning? That's a cultural shift. And now they're saying BBC One audio from 12pm to 9pm? That's prime time radio right there. Could be nice to have some structured programming back on the air.

User20: BroadcastBuzz
Posted: Date at 09:15 AM
I’m curious how they’ll transition between these wildly different formats. Looks like the Krash audio fun will be designated to the graveyard shift, from 9pm to 6am. Could be a way to keep the whimsy of the airwaves alive while satisfying all listener demographics.

User29: CymruAmByth
Posted: Date at 09:20 AM
Finally, some Welsh representation with the S4C segment! It’s good Nart Media is looking to cater to different audiences. Hopefully, this also means increased support for Welsh language programming.

User22: TheRadioHistorian
Posted: Date at 09:25 AM
This is an innovative approach by Nart Media. They're shifting from a music service to an audio broadcast trio. 231 AM is proving to be a versatile frequency. This planned schedule is like a nod to the old days of diverse radio programming.

User1: DJMike
Posted: Date at 09:30 AM
I've got mixed feelings. The music was a huge draw for me, but the diversity is nice. I just hope the transitions between S4C, BBC One, and Krash are smooth. It's like three stations in one. Could be a game-changer or a hot mess!

User30: VintageVibes
Posted: Date at 09:35 AM
Nart Media is really turning 231 AM into a full-service station. But I'm feeling nostalgic over the disappearance of the music-centric focus. I’ll miss those tunes, even though I enjoyed the Ofcom gig and chuckled at Krash's antics.

User21: AirwavesAnalyst
Posted: Date at 09:40 AM
Strategic move by Nart Media to keep 231 AM relevant. A variety format appeals to a broader range, and timed broadcasts can also gather specific audiences. I'm eager to gauge listener reactions once everything is live.

User31: LateNightChuckler
Posted: Date at 09:45 AM
Krash the Rabbit during the nighttime? Perfect! His routine is a staple of my nights now. As for daytime slots, sounds like a cultural buffet on radio. Exciting times for 231 AM – reserving judgment until I see it in action!

(Following replies continue discussing the potential impact of the new broadcast format plan submitted by Nart Media, with users expressing their anticipation, skepticism, and curiosity about how the changes will shape the listening experience on 231 AM.)

Write a blog post about "Great Radio's departure from AM" and that Nart did this to "reserve the frequency for something different" and that a select few songs played in succession for several weeks, then there was a day of Ofcom/Arqiva engineering loops and then the real fun began

Title: Tuning Out the Past: Great Radio's End and Nart's New Beginning on AM

When Great Radio announced its departure from the traditional AM band, loyal listeners across the spectrum felt the bittersweet pangs of progress. For years, 231 AM had been synonymous with a musical journey through time, a constant companion for many, providing a backdrop to life's many moments. But as the station bid adieu to its long-standing frequency, a new chapter was about to unfold—Nart Media had reserved the frequency for a different kind of experiment, marking the end of an era and the beginning of an eclectic audio adventure.

In the last weeks of Great Radio's presence on AM, a select few songs echoed repeatedly across the airwaves. These tracks—each holding a special place in the pantheon of rock and pop classics—formed a sort of "Song Filled Goodbye," a greatest hits mixtape that paid homage to the station's legacy. As each song reverberated one final time, it was as if the radio alone knew the weight of the silence that would follow.

Then, 231 AM was gripped by an eerie calm. A single day followed where the only sounds were the monotonous loops of Ofcom and Arqiva engineering announcements—an interlude, filling the gap before the frequency found its new voice. Ofcom's messages detailed their regulatory role and ambitions, while Arqiva's focused on infrastructure and technicalities—they were placeholders, primers for the uninitiated on the intricacies of radio transmission, building anticipation for what was to come.

After this brief intermission, the real fun began. In a twist no one saw coming, Krash the Rabbit, a vibrant character from the animated world of Kikoriki, flipped the script, bringing a "Fun Filled Krash" to the otherwise silent channel. Jokes, fake conversations, and playful antics replaced the somber tunes of the past. This 30-minute loop of levity was refreshed every few days, providing a nightly reprieve from the day's worries and a sense of unexpected companionship during the small hours.

This metamorphosis of 231 AM, masterminded by Nart Media, was as confusing as it was delightful. The shift from music to merry mayhem represented more than just a format change; it was a strategic move to reserve the frequency until something more permanent could take root. Meanwhile, Krash's capers provided a stopgap of smiles—and perhaps a testing ground for the audience's receptiveness to novelty.

Now, months later, as rumours swirl about a possible relay of BBC One audio, and a schedule divided between S4C and Krash’s comedy, Nart Media’s vision for a dynamic, diverse listening experience comes clearer into focus. Their placeholder may have evolved beyond its original purview, spawning a radio variety show unlike any other.

The full implications of this switch-up for 231 AM remain to be seen. From the "Song Filled Goodbye" to the engineering loops, and through to the "Fun Filled Krash," this frequency's journey has been anything but predictable. And while the loss of Great Radio on the AM band leaves a hole in the hearts of its dedicated fans, the unfolding story keeps those dials turning and ears perked, eagerly tuned to what's next. Whether for better or worse, 231 AM reminds us all that radio, much like life, is ever-changing and full of surprises.
Write that the final day of the song goodbye had a vague hint to what was to come, the Arqiva shameless recruitment drive had a similar hint

Title: The Final Farewell and Hidden Hints: Tracing the Future in Great Radio's Last Days

The closing chapter of Great Radio on the 231 AM frequency was a carefully crafted homage, a "Song Filled Goodbye" that served not only as a tribute to its storied history but also as a subtle conduit to the future. As the last day of broadcasts unfurled, keen listeners may have picked up on something—vague hints embedded within the playlist that whispered of what was to come.

It's as if each selected song carried within its lyrics or perhaps in the DJ’s transitions, a cryptic nod to the imminent changes. Was it in the rebellious riff of "Bad to the Bone" or the heartfelt homesickness of "Sweet Home Alabama"? Did "Paint It Black" suggest a blank canvas awaiting new artistry, or was the driving beat of "Seven Nation Army" heralding the arrival of an eclectic new force? The clues were there, elusive and faint, yet present enough to stir curiosity and speculation.

Then came the daylong Arqiva loop, which to many seemed nothing more than a straightforward engineering and recruitment drive. However, amidst the promotion and technical jargon lay similar hints—a certain emphasis on innovation or an offhand remark about the diverse potential of radio frequencies. It wasn't just about filling roles; it was about shaping the future of broadcasting. This "shameless recruitment drive" now appeared as a cheeky foreshadowing of a frequency revitalized, a teaser of Arqiva's role in laying the groundwork for whatever was waiting in the wings.

These breadcrumbs, once esoteric, now manifest in the form of Krash the Rabbit's comedic takeover. This placeholder programming, initially perceived as a mere quirky interlude, perhaps held a greater purpose in Nart Media's grand plan. The script was flipped, but not without prior notice—for those listening closely enough, the signals were there, suggesting this rabbit had more up his sleeve than we all anticipated.

In retrospect, the recurring theme of transformation and renewal ties back to these initial Easter eggs. The "Fun Filled Krash" was not just a random act of broadcast whimsy—it was a calculated step in a larger strategy. Nart Media's follow-up notice to Ofcom requesting a change in the station’s format, and the subsequent discussions about a diversified schedule encapsulating S4C, BBC One, and the Krash comedy blocks all seem to align with the earlier hinted narrative.

While Great Radio's AM swansong felt like a poignant conclusion, it also played the enigmatic prelude to an intriguing broadcast evolution. As 231 AM continues to surprise and enthrall its audience with a chameleon-like propensity for change, we're reminded of the fluid nature of the radio medium. From a legacy of music to a beacon of comedic relief, and with whispers of traditional network relays, 231 AM time and again tunes into the unexpected, charting a course where the only constant is reinvention.

Write the script of the retune notice beginning with the final few words of "Paint it Black" and then explaining what has happened, and ending with a "Stay tuned for something funny, from Kra-, uh, You'll have to wait to find out" and then the first few words of "Amish Paradise"

[The ambient sounds of the final guitar strums of "Paint It Black" by The Rolling Stones fade slowly into the background.]

Voiceover: [Warm, friendly, and with a touch of nostalgia] "...turning your world to black." And as the last color from this classic fades, we close the curtain on an era filled with music that painted our lives with every shade of emotion. This is Great Radio, your faithful companion through decades of sound. 

[Pause for effect]

Voiceover: But as we leave the airwaves of 231 AM, know that this is not the end. It's merely a change in frequency, a turn of the dial to something new and unexpected. Great Radio may be stepping aside, but the show will go on. 

[Small, reflective pause]

Voiceover: In its place, an unexpected chorus of laughter is set to fill the void. The past weeks have echoed with the familiar tunes that shaped our station, culminating in an eclectic loop so many of you came to love—a final tip of the hat, a nod to what has always been central to our spirit: joy. 

[Hint of amusement in the voice]

Voiceover: But the silence that followed was merely a breath, taken before diving into the deep end of comedy. From an Ofcom message of recruitment, a clear segue if you listened closely, came a leap into the whimsical world of 231 AM's very own placeholder broadcaster. 

[A knowing chuckle]

Voiceover: So, as we retune our dials and find new frequencies to call home, stay tuned for something funny, something fresh, something... well, let's just say it's going to be a hoot! 

[Build anticipation]

Voiceover: From Kra-, uh, it seems I've said too much already. You'll just have to wait to find out who—

[Sudden quirky intro beats to "Amish Paradise" by "Weird Al" Yankovic begin]

Voiceover: And with that, allow your imagination to graze in the pastures of "Amish Paradise." Thanks for the memories, listeners. Make sure to keep your ears open and your hearts ready for laughs.

[The parodic lyrics of "Amish Paradise" kick in]

"Weird Al" Yankovic: [Singing] As I walk through the valley where I harvest my grain...

[The radio script signs off, leaving listeners with a humorous transition from a music legend to a beloved parody artist, hinting at the comedic tone ahead.]
